question.text: Which category of Alternative Investment Funds (AIFs) can be either open-ended or closed-ended as per SEBI regulations?

answer.text: All categories of AIFs must be closed-ended

explanation.text: **The Logic:** Under SEBI AIF Regulations, Category I and Category II AIFs must be closed-ended. Only Category III AIFs (which include hedge funds and funds employing diverse trading strategies) can be either open-ended or closed-ended. **The Trap:** Option D sounds plausible because most AIFs are closed-ended, but Category III is the exception. Candidates who remember "AIFs are closed-ended" as a blanket rule get this wrong. **Cross-Exam Context:** NISM Series XIX-A (AIF) is the dedicated AIF certification. NISM Series V-A (MF Distributors) also briefly covers AIF classifications. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Category III AIFs (hedge funds) need the flexibility of open-ended structures for their trading strategies, which is why SEBI allows this exception. Categories I and II invest in illiquid assets requiring lock-in periods.
